title: Implement a Character Class for a Role-Playing Game

content.problemStatement:
In this task, you will implement a character class for a role-playing game using multiple inheritance. The character should have attributes and abilities from different classes: Warrior, Mage, and Rogue.

Create a base class called Character with basic attributes like name and level. Then create three classes: Warrior (with strength attribute), Mage (with intelligence attribute), and Rogue (with agility attribute). Finally, create a PlayerCharacter class that inherits from all these classes using multiple inheritance.

The PlayerCharacter class should have a method called display_stats() that shows all the character's attributes.

Example test case:
Input:
Elara
5

Output:
Name: Elara
Level: 5
Strength: 10
Intelligence: 10
Agility: 10

Your task is to implement the classes and the multiple inheritance structure to create the PlayerCharacter class.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line is a string name (the character’s name, length ≤ 100, may contain spaces), the second line is an integer level (1 ≤ level ≤ 100).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output five lines in the exact order: "Name: <name>", "Level: <level>", "Strength: 10", "Intelligence: 10", "Agility: 10". Test cases are printed consecutively without blank lines between them.

sampleTestCases.explanation: One test case: name "Elara" and level 5 are read, a PlayerCharacter is created and its five attributes are printed.
Two test cases are processed sequentially, each producing its own five‑line block.
Names may contain spaces; the whole line is taken as the character name.

hidden.label: general case 2
hidden.expectedOutput:
Name: X
Level: 5
Strength: 10
Intelligence: 10
Agility: 10
Name: Y Z
Level: 6
Strength: 10
Intelligence: 10
Agility: 10
Name: Long Name With Spaces
Level: 7
Strength: 10
Intelligence: 10
Agility: 10

solution.approach: Read the integer T. Loop T times. For each iteration read the whole line containing the name (using getline or equivalent to keep spaces) and then read the integer level. The character’s combat attributes are fixed to 10, so no computation is required beyond printing. The method display_stats() (or its equivalent) simply prints the stored name, level and the three constant attributes. The algorithm runs in O(T) time and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time output per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored

code.Python3:
import sys
def main():
    data=sys.stdin.read().splitlines()
    if not data:
        return
    t=int(data[0])
    idx=1
    out=[]
    for _ in range(t):
        name=data[idx]
        level=int(data[idx+1])
        idx+=2
        out.append(f"Name: {name}")
        out.append(f"Level: {level}")
        out.append("Strength: 10")
        out.append("Intelligence: 10")
        out.append("Agility: 10")
    sys.stdout.write("\n".join(out))
if __name__=="__main__":
    main()
